stantly encouraged us to complete this work. v ABSTRACT Low-density parity check (LDPC) codes are an attractive error correction scheme for ensuring data integrity in new generation of memories. This project introduces compact hardware architecture for a high-speed Low-Density Parity-Check (LDPC) decoder designed for 50G Passive Optical Network (50G-PON) applications. The proposed architecture focuses on achieving very high decoding throughput with strong error-correction performance while minim

uses on achieving very high decoding throughput with strong error-correction performance while minimizing hardware complexity and resource consumption. A phased decoding strategy is adopted to optimize the tradeoff between processing speed and implementation efficiency. By utilizing the quasicyclic characteristics of the LDPC parity-check matrix, a fixed and regular interconnection structure between check nodes and variable nodes is realized, significantly simplifying hardware routing. Furthermo

en check nodes and variable nodes is realized, significantly simplifying hardware routing. Furthermore, a block-level column cyclic shift technique is applied to the parity-check matrix to reduce control overhead and improve parallelism. Simplified message normalization and quantization methods are employed to lower computational complexity without compromising decoding accuracy. The decoder is implemented using a pipelined processing architecture to support continuous high-speed operation. Expe

plemented using a pipelined processing architecture to support continuous high-speed operation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed design achieves a decoding throughput of 49.7664 Gbps on a single FPGA, making it a practical and efficient solution for nextgeneration 50G-PON systems. vi TABLE OF CONTENTS CHAPTER TITLE PAGE NO. e continuous expansion of broadband applications has significantly increased the demand for high-speed and reliable communication systems. Modern users require seamless connectivity for data-intensive services such as video streaming, cloud computing, and real-time communication. In this context, the emergence of 50G Passive Optical Network (50G-PON) technology plays a crucial role in enabling multi-gigabit data transmission in optical access networks. Error-control coding techniques are therefore essential to maintain data integrity. Among these techniques, L

trol coding techniques are therefore essential to maintain data integrity. Among these techniques, Low-Density Parity-Check Codes (LDPC) codes have gained widespread attention because of their excellent error-correction capability and near Shannon limit performance. These codes are highly efficient in minimizing bit error rates, especially in low signal-to-noise ratio conditions. 1.3 Comparison of LDPC and Turbo Codes Both LDPC codes and Turbo Codes are considered near Shannon limit coding techn

LDPC decoding algorithms support a higher degree of parallelism, which enables faster processing. In addition, they exhibit lower decoding latency and reduced implementation complexity. Unlike turbo codes, LDPC codes do not suffer from error floors at high signal-to-noise ratios, making them more s

codes, LDPC codes do not suffer from error floors at high signal-to-noise ratios, making them more suitable for high-speed communication systems. 1.4 Challenges in LDPC Decoder Implementation Despite their advantages, implementing LDPC decoders in hardware presents several challenges. The sparse nature of the parity-check matrix leads to irregular interconnections, which complicates the design. Additionally, LDPC decoders require large memory resources to store intermediate messages during itera

ditionally, LDPC decoders require large memory resources to store intermediate messages during iterative decoding. These factors increase the overall hardware complexity and make it difficult to achieve high throughput in real-time applications, particularly in VLSI implementations. 1.5 LDPC Decoding Algorithms LDPC codes are typically decoded using iterative message-passing algorithms. To address this issue, simplified versions such as the Offset Min-Sum (MS) algorithm have been developed. The Offset MS algorithm approximates the SP algorithm while significantly reducing computational complexity by eliminating nonlinear operations, making it more suitable for hardware implementation. 3 1.6 Scheduling Techniques

Two primary scheduling methods are commonly employed: flooding scheduling and layered scheduling. In flooding scheduling, all nodes are updated simultaneously in each iteration, whereas layered scheduling processes nodes sequentially. day noncritical systems, where engineers must design reliable systems on inherently unreliable hardware fabrics. With the increasing complexity of modern System- onChip (SoC) designs, new interconnection strategies such as Network-on-Chip (NoC) have been introduced to enable scalable communication among a large 7 number of processing elements. on processing components, the interconnection network itself has now become a significant portion of system resources and is equally vulnerable to faults. As semiconductor device geometries continue to scale down, the reliability of hardware components decreases, leading to a higher probability of faults occurring even after manufacturing. NoC One of the primary approaches to improving reliability in NoC systems is hardware redundancy, which provides fault tolerance by replicating hardware components. Techniques such as Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) utilize multiple instances of the same hardware along with a voting mechanism to ensure correct operation even 8 in the presence of faults.
